(German: *Haus Lothringen*) was a [noble house](/wiki/Noble_house_(term) "Noble house (term)") that ruled over the [Duchy of Lorraine](/wiki/Duchy_of_Lorraine "Duchy of Lorraine") (1047-1431, 1473-1737) within the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), and also held other feudal domains, such as the [County of Vaudémont](/wiki/Counts_of_Vaud%C3%A9mont "Counts of Vaudémont"), the [Duchy of Bar](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bar "Duchy of Bar"), the [Duchy of Guise](/wiki/Dukes_of_Guise "Dukes of Guise"), and various minor possessions. The senior ducal branch died out in 1431, but the cadet branch, headed by the [Counts of Vaudémont](/wiki/Counts_of_Vaud%C3%A9mont "Counts of Vaudémont"), continued and reacquired the Duchy of Lorraine in 1473, thus establishing the junior ducal line, that ruled over Lorraine until 1737. Its cadet branch, the [House of Guise](/wiki/House_of_Guise "House of Guise"), played a prominent role in political history of the [Kingdom of France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKiblerZinn2011560-561-1)

The **House of Lorraine** (German: *Haus Lothringen*) was a [noble house](/wiki/Noble_house_(term) "Noble house (term)") that ruled over the [Duchy of Lorraine](/wiki/Duchy_of_Lorraine "Duchy of Lorraine") (1047-1431, 1473-1737) within the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), and also held other feudal domains, such as the [County of Vaudémont](/wiki/Counts_of_Vaud%C3%A9mont "Counts of Vaudémont"), the [Duchy of Bar](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bar "Duchy of Bar"), the [Duchy of Guise](/wiki/Dukes_of_Guise "Dukes of Guise"), and various minor possessions. The senior ducal branch died out in 1431, but the cadet branch, headed by the [Counts of Vaudémont](/wiki/Counts_of_Vaud%C3%A9mont "Counts of Vaudémont"), continued and reacquired the Duchy of Lorraine in 1473, thus establishing the junior ducal line, that ruled over Lorraine until 1737. Its cadet branch, the [House of Guise](/wiki/House_of_Guise "House of Guise"), played a prominent role in political history of the [Kingdom of France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKiblerZinn2011560-561-1)

By the marriage of duke [Francis of Lorraine](/wiki/Francis_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Francis I, Holy Roman Emperor") to [Maria Theresa](/wiki/Maria_Theresa "Maria Theresa") of Austria in 1736, and with the success in the ensuing [War of the Austrian Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Austrian_Succession "War of the Austrian Succession") (1740–1748), the House of Lorraine was joined to the [House of Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") and became known as the [House of Habsburg-Lorraine](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg-Lorraine "House of Habsburg-Lorraine") (German: *Haus Habsburg-Lothringen*). Francis, his sons [Joseph II](/wiki/Joseph_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Joseph II, Holy Roman Emperor") and [Leopold II](/wiki/Leopold_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Leopold II, Holy Roman Emperor"), and his grandson [Francis II](/wiki/Francis_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Francis II, Holy Roman Emperor") were the last four [Holy Roman emperors](/wiki/Holy_Roman_emperor "Holy Roman emperor") from 1745 until the [dissolution of the empire](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Holy_Roman_Empire "Dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire") in 1806. The House of Habsburg-Lorraine inherited the [Habsburg Empire](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"), ruling the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") and then [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") until the [dissolution of the monarchy](/wiki/Austria-Hungary#Dissolution "Austria-Hungary") in 1918.

Although its senior [agnates](/wiki/Agnates "Agnates") are the [dukes of Hohenberg](/wiki/Hohenberg_family "Hohenberg family"), the house is currently headed by [Karl von Habsburg](/wiki/Karl_von_Habsburg "Karl von Habsburg") (born 1961), grandson of the last emperor [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_Austria "Charles I of Austria").[[2]](#cite_note-Shepherd-2)

## Ancestry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lorraine&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Ancestry")]

Lineage of the House of Lorraine is certain since the 11th century, but due to the fragmentary nature of [historical sources](/wiki/Historical_sources "Historical sources"), reliable tracing of its older ancestry proved to be challenging within [genealogical studies](/wiki/Genealogical_studies "Genealogical studies"), particularly in light of various dynastic claims (direct or indirect connections with the [Carolingians](/wiki/Carolingians "Carolingians"); common ancestry with the [Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburgs "Habsburgs")) posed by the junior ducal line during the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period").

### A controversial origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lorraine&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: A controversial origin")]

The main two theories of the House's origin are:

* the theory of [Etichonid](/wiki/Etichonids "Etichonids") ancestry, which claims that [Adalbert of Metz](/wiki/Adalbert,_Duke_of_Lorraine "Adalbert, Duke of Lorraine") and his brother [Gerard](/wiki/Gerard,_Duke_of_Lorraine "Gerard, Duke of Lorraine") were descendants of the Nordgau branch of the Etichonid Dynasty, the same branch from which the [House of Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") and the [House of Zähringen](/wiki/House_of_Z%C3%A4hringen "House of Zähringen") could possibly descend;[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)
* the theory of [Gerardide](/wiki/Gerard_I_of_Paris "Gerard I of Paris") ancestry, which claims that Adalbert and Gerard descended from the [Matfridings](/wiki/Matfriding "Matfriding") which are thought to have been a branch of the [Gerardides](/wiki/Gerard_I_of_Paris "Gerard I of Paris").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

What is more securely demonstrated is that in 1048 [Emperor Henry III](/wiki/Emperor_Henry_III "Emperor Henry III") gave the Duchy of [Upper Lorraine](/wiki/Upper_Lorraine "Upper Lorraine") first to [Adalbert of Metz](/wiki/Adalbert,_Duke_of_Lorraine "Adalbert, Duke of Lorraine") and then to his brother [Gerard](/wiki/Gerard,_Duke_of_Lorraine "Gerard, Duke of Lorraine") whose successors (collectively known as the House of Alsace or the House of Châtenois) retained the duchy until the death of [Charles the Bold](/wiki/Charles_II,_Duke_of_Lorraine "Charles II, Duke of Lorraine") in 1431.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKiblerZinn2011561-4)

#### Certainties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lorraine&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Certainties")]

Based on documents dating from the 11th century, researchers are able to establish the two generations preceding Gerard of Alsace. The oldest certain generation is a sibling group shown at the top of the family chart below composed of Gerard, Adalbert and Adelaide:[[5]](#cite_note-ES-5)

:   Gerard, Count, probably of Metz, died between 1021 and 1033, married Eve, daughter of Count Sigefroid, ancestor of the Counts of Luxembourg. From this marriage were born two children: Sigfried, died between 1017 and 1020, and Berscinde, abbess of Remiremont;

:   Adelaide married Henry of Franconia, Count in Wormsgau, and is the mother of Emperor Conrad II the Salic and several other children;

:   Adalbert was Count of Metz, died in 1037. A donation dated June 12, 1037 in favor of the Abbey of Saint-Mathieu calls him dux and marchio Lotoringie. He married Judith who gave birth to a single son, named Gérard.[[5]](#cite_note-ES-5)

:   Gérard, son of Adalbert, died in 1045. He is called Count of Alsace in a charter of May 1038 where he puts an end to a dispute against the Abbey of Remiremont. He married a Gisèle, who gave birth to Adalbert, Duke of Lorraine in 1047, Gérard, Count of Metz, then Duke of Lorraine in 1047, Conrad, Adalberon, Beatrix, Odelric, Cuno, Oda, abbess of Remiremont from 1048 to 1071, Azelinus, Ida and Adelheid, as stated in the Notitiæ Fundationis Monasterii Bosonis-Villæ (Notice of the founding of the monastery of Bouzonville).[[5]](#cite_note-ES-5)

#### Etichonid

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lorraine&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Etichonid")]

The [Etichonid](/wiki/Etichonids "Etichonids") origin was unanimously recognized from the 18th until the 20th century. For this reason, the marriage between Maria Theresa of Austria and Francis of Lorraine was seen at the time as the reunion of the two branches of the dynasty. The main proponents of this theory have been: [Dom Calmet](/wiki/Antoine_Augustin_Calmet "Antoine Augustin Calmet") (1672 † 1757),[[6]](#cite_note-6) [Nicolas Viton de Saint-Allais](/wiki/Nicolas_Viton_de_Saint-Allais "Nicolas Viton de Saint-Allais") (1773 † 1842)[[7]](#cite_note-7) and more recently [Michel Dugast Rouillé](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michel_Dugast_Rouill%C3%A9 "fr:Michel Dugast Rouillé") (1919 † 1987)[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) and Henry Bogdan.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

#### Gerardide-Matfriding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lorraine&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Gerardide-Matfriding")]

The main proponents of the Gerardide-Matfriding theory are: Eduard Hlawitschka,[[9]](#cite_note-Hlawitschka-9) George Poull[[10]](#cite_note-Poull-10) and partially the *[Europäische Stammtafeln](/wiki/Europ%C3%A4ische_Stammtafeln "Europäische Stammtafeln")* (which however does not take into account the kinship with the Girardides).[[5]](#cite_note-ES-5)

:   The question of the origin of the House of Lorraine was taken up in the 20th Century by [Eduard Hlawitschk](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eduard_Hlawitschka "de:Eduard Hlawitschka"). He established that Gérard d'Alsace came from the [Counts of Metz](/wiki/List_of_Counts_of_Metz "List of Counts of Metz"), but grouped these counts into two family groups: the [Matfried](/wiki/Matfriede "Matfriede") of the 9th Century, and the Adalbert/Gérard of the 10th century. The absence of documentation does not allow a formal kinship to be established between the two groups. But he notes that this kinship is very probable because they are of similar status, use the same onomastic background and have concluded matrimonial alliances in the same circles (House of Ardennes, imperial families, etc.).

:   Eduard Hlawitschka continues his proposal by estimating that the Matfrieds could even be descended from the [Gerardide](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Girardides "fr:Girardides") (family of Gérard/Girard, Leuthard, Alard/Adal(h)ard, Beggo/Bégon/Bérenger), [counts of Paris](/wiki/Counts_of_Paris "Counts of Paris") at the end of the 8th century and the beginning of the 9th century.[[9]](#cite_note-Hlawitschka-9) His thesis was taken up by the historian George Poull[[10]](#cite_note-Poull-10) and partially by the *Europäische Stammtafeln* (which however do not take into account the kinship with the Girardides).[[5]](#cite_note-ES-5) Eduard Hlawitschka's thesis, with the Girardide connection, is the one that currently meets with the most support, while the Etichonide thesis seems to be receding (for the [agnateic](/wiki/Agnate "Agnate")/male lineage, of course; in the [cognatic](/w/index.php?title=Cognate_(genealogy)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cognate (genealogy) (page does not exist)")/female lineage, there are obviously relationships, at least with the [Eguisheim-Nordgau](/w/index.php?title=List_of_counts_of_Nordgau&action=edit&redlink=1 "List of counts of Nordgau (page does not exist)")).

#### Renaissance & Modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lorraine&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Renaissance & Modern")]

The [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") dukes of Lorraine tended to arrogate to themselves claims to [Carolingian](/wiki/Carolingian "Carolingian") ancestry, as illustrated by [Alexandre Dumas, père](/wiki/Alexandre_Dumas,_p%C3%A8re "Alexandre Dumas, père") in the novel *[La Dame de Monsoreau](/wiki/La_Dame_de_Monsoreau "La Dame de Monsoreau")* (1846);[[12]](#cite_note-12) in fact, as seen above, the only evidence is of some interraltionships with the [Carolingians](/wiki/Carolingians "Carolingians"), and some intermarriages with the [Saxon Emperors](/wiki/Ottonian_dynasty "Ottonian dynasty") and the [Salian Emperors](/wiki/Salian_Dynasty "Salian Dynasty"), but not so much as to give them any claims to the [Imperial Crown](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor"), much less the [Crown of France](/wiki/King_of_France "King of France").

This did not stop the [Bourbons](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon "House of Bourbon") in the [Treaty of Montmartre](/wiki/Treaty_of_Montmartre "Treaty of Montmartre") in 1662 naming the non-Capetian House of Lorraine based on this supposed ancestry as next in line to the French throne after the Bourbons, in preference to the [Capetian House of Courtenay](/wiki/Capetian_House_of_Courtenay "Capetian House of Courtenay"), who would have been next by the Salic Law. Based on the terms of the accord, Louis XIV was given control of the [Duchy of Lorraine](/wiki/Duchy_of_Lorraine "Duchy of Lorraine"), and [Charles IV](/wiki/Charles_IV,_Duke_of_Lorraine "Charles IV, Duke of Lorraine")'s family would become princes in the French royal family. However, it failed to take hold and Charles IV publicly repudiated it.[[13]](#cite_note-spangler-13)[[14]](#cite_note-lipp-14)

The house of Vaudémont continued to rule the independent duchies of Lorraine and Bar. However, [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV")'s imperialist ambitions (which involved the occupation of Lorraine in 1669–97) forced the dukes into a permanent alliance with his archenemies, the [Holy Roman Emperors](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperors "Holy Roman Emperors") from the [House of Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg").

The final status of the duchy was only determined as part of the settlement of the [War of Austrian Succession](/wiki/War_of_Austrian_Succession "War of Austrian Succession"). As part of that, [Francis Stephen](/wiki/Francis_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Francis I, Holy Roman Emperor"), now Holy Roman Emperor and husband of [Maria Theresa of Austria](/wiki/Maria_Theresa_of_Austria "Maria Theresa of Austria"), Queen of Bohemia and Hungary, surrendered the [Duchy of Lorraine](/wiki/Duchy_of_Lorraine "Duchy of Lorraine") to France (to be ruled by the former [King of Poland](/wiki/King_of_Poland "King of Poland"), [Stanisław Leszczyński](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Leszczy%C5%84ski "Stanisław Leszczyński"), the father in law of [Louis XV of France](/wiki/Louis_XV_of_France "Louis XV of France"), during his lifetime, and then to be inherited by France. Francis Stephen and his heirs received the [Grand Duchy of Tuscany](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Tuscany "Grand Duchy of Tuscany"), and all his wife's Habsburg lands ([House of Habsburg-Lorraine](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg-Lorraine "House of Habsburg-Lorraine")).

## Senior ducal line

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lorraine&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Senior ducal line")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/C._1350_Luxembourg_and_Lotharingia.jpg/250px-C._1350_Luxembourg_and_Lotharingia.jpg)](/wiki/File:C._1350_Luxembourg_and_Lotharingia.jpg)

[Duchy of Lorraine](/wiki/Duchy_of_Lorraine "Duchy of Lorraine") (blue) in the middle of the 14th century

The senior ducal line of the House emerged upon acquiring the [Duchy of Upper Lorraine](/wiki/Duchy_of_Upper_Lorraine "Duchy of Upper Lorraine") in the middle of the 11th century. As a loyal subject of the emperor [Henry III](/wiki/Henry_III,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Henry III, Holy Roman Emperor"), count [Adalbert of Metz](/wiki/Adalbert,_Duke_of_Upper_Lorraine "Adalbert, Duke of Upper Lorraine") was appointed as [Duke of Upper Lorraine](/wiki/Duke_of_Upper_Lorraine "Duke of Upper Lorraine") in 1047, but died already in 1048. His brother [Gerard](/wiki/Gerard,_Duke_of_Upper_Lorraine "Gerard, Duke of Upper Lorraine") (d. 1070) was addpointed to succeede him as the new duke, and was in turn succeeded by his direct male descendants, who ruled over the Duchy up to 1431. During that period, the Duchy was one of the most prominet states of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2016198-199-15) In 1431, the duke [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II,_Duke_of_Lorraine "Charles II, Duke of Lorraine") died without sons or brothers. His closest male relative was [Antoine, Count of Vaudémont](/wiki/Antoine,_Count_of_Vaud%C3%A9mont "Antoine, Count of Vaudémont"), from the cadet branch of the House, but the Duchy of Lorraine passed to Charles' daughter [Isabella](/wiki/Isabella,_Duchess_of_Lorraine "Isabella, Duchess of Lorraine"), and her husband [René I](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_of_Anjou "René of Anjou") from the [House of Valois-Anjou](/wiki/House_of_Valois-Anjou "House of Valois-Anjou"), thus initiating a Vaudémont-Angevine conflict ower Lorraine that lasted for years.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKiblerZinn2011795-16)

## Junior ducal line

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lorraine&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Junior ducal line")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Joinville_Ch%C3%A2teau_Grand_Jardin.jpg/250px-Joinville_Ch%C3%A2teau_Grand_Jardin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joinville_Ch%C3%A2teau_Grand_Jardin.jpg)

The [Château du Grand Jardin](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_du_Grand_Jardin "Château du Grand Jardin") in [Joinville](/wiki/Joinville,_Haute-Marne "Joinville, Haute-Marne"), the seat of the [Counts and Dukes of Guise](/wiki/Counts_and_Dukes_of_Guise "Counts and Dukes of Guise").

See also: [House of Guise](/wiki/House_of_Guise "House of Guise")

Since the extinction of the senior ducal line in 1431, the cadet branch of the House, headed by [Counts of Vaudémont](/wiki/Counts_of_Vaud%C3%A9mont "Counts of Vaudémont"), took ower the family claims and aspirations, engaging in a series of disputes and conflicts with the Angevins over the possession of Lorraine. Those disputes were resolved in 1445, when count [Frederick II of Vaudémont](/wiki/Frederick_II,_Count_of_Vaud%C3%A9mont "Frederick II, Count of Vaudémont") married [Yolande](/wiki/Yolande,_Duchess_of_Lorraine "Yolande, Duchess of Lorraine"), daughter of Isabella and René, thus establishing a close conection with the [Angevine](/wiki/House_of_Valois-Anjou "House of Valois-Anjou") dukes of Lorraine. In 1470, Yolande's brother, duke [John II of Lorraine](/wiki/John_II,_Duke_of_Lorraine "John II, Duke of Lorraine") died and was succeeded by his son [Nicholas I](/wiki/Nicholas_I,_Duke_of_Lorraine "Nicholas I, Duke of Lorraine"), who died already in 1473, without male heirs. Thus, Yolande's and Frederick's son, count [René of Vaudémont](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_II,_Duke_of_Lorraine "René II, Duke of Lorraine") claimed the Duchy of Lorraine and succeeded in reacquiring it for the family, thus establishing the junior ducal line of the House, and later also adding to his titles that of the [Duke of Bar](/wiki/Duke_of_Bar "Duke of Bar") (a domian within the French realm).[[17]](#cite_note-17)

During the entire period of their rule in Lorraine (1473-1737), dukes from the junior line were facing constant challenges from both the imperial authority, and the neighboring [Kingdom of France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France"), thus leading to a series of political and territorial disputes, conflicts, and arrangements with both sides. In 1542, the Treaty of Nuremberg was reached with the Emperor, securing a high degree of autonomy for the Duchy.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2016230-18)

In the same time, the [French Wars of Religion](/wiki/French_Wars_of_Religion "French Wars of Religion") saw the rise of a cadet branch of the Lorraine ducal family, the [House of Guise](/wiki/House_of_Guise "House of Guise"), which became a dominant force in French politics and, during the later years of [Henry III](/wiki/Henry_III_of_France "Henry III of France")'s reign, was on the verge of succeeding to the throne of France.[[19]](#cite_note-19) [Mary of Guise](/wiki/Mary_of_Guise "Mary of Guise"), mother of [Mary, Queen of Scots](/wiki/Mary,_Queen_of_Scots "Mary, Queen of Scots"), also came from this family. Under the [Bourbon](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon "House of Bourbon") monarchy the remaining branch of the House of Guise, headed by the [duc d'Elbeuf](/wiki/Lords,_Marquesses_and_Dukes_of_Elbeuf "Lords, Marquesses and Dukes of Elbeuf"), remained part of the highest ranks of French aristocracy.

## House of Habsburg‑Lorraine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lorraine&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: House of Habsburg‑Lorraine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Imperial_Coat_of_Arms_of_the_Empire_of_Austria_%281815%29.svg/250px-Imperial_Coat_of_Arms_of_the_Empire_of_Austria_%281815%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Imperial_Coat_of_Arms_of_the_Empire_of_Austria_(1815).svg)

The coat of arms of the House of Habsburg-Lorraine. The shield displays the marshaled arms of the Habsburg, Babenberg and Lorraine families.

Main article: [House of Habsburg-Lorraine](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg-Lorraine "House of Habsburg-Lorraine")

See also: [House of Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg"), [House of Hohenberg](/wiki/House_of_Hohenberg "House of Hohenberg"), and [Austria-Este](/wiki/Austria-Este "Austria-Este")

After [Emperor Joseph I](/wiki/Emperor_Joseph_I "Emperor Joseph I") and [Emperor Charles VI](/wiki/Emperor_Charles_VI "Emperor Charles VI") failed to produce a son and heir, the [Pragmatic Sanction of 1713](/wiki/Pragmatic_Sanction_of_1713 "Pragmatic Sanction of 1713") left the throne to the latter's yet unborn daughter, [Maria Theresa](/wiki/Maria_Theresa "Maria Theresa"). In 1736 Emperor Charles arranged her marriage to [Francis of Lorraine](/wiki/Francis_of_Lorraine "Francis of Lorraine") who agreed to exchange his hereditary lands for the [Grand Duchy of Tuscany](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Tuscany "Grand Duchy of Tuscany") (as well as the [Duchy of Teschen](/wiki/Duchy_of_Teschen "Duchy of Teschen") from the Emperor).

At Charles's death in 1740 the Habsburg holdings passed to Maria Theresa and Francis, who was later elected (in 1745) Holy Roman Emperor as Francis I. The Habsburg-Lorraine nuptials and [dynastic union](/wiki/Dynastic_union "Dynastic union") precipitated, and survived, the [War of the Austrian Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Austrian_Succession "War of the Austrian Succession"). Francis and Maria Theresa's daughters [Marie Antoinette](/wiki/Marie_Antoinette "Marie Antoinette") and [Maria Carolina of Austria](/wiki/Maria_Carolina_of_Austria "Maria Carolina of Austria") became Queens of France and [Naples-Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily "Kingdom of Sicily"), respectively, while their sons [Joseph II](/wiki/Joseph_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Joseph II, Holy Roman Emperor") and [Leopold II](/wiki/Leopold_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Leopold II, Holy Roman Emperor") succeeded to the imperial title.

Apart from the core Habsburg dominions, including the triple crowns of [Austria](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire"), [Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary_(1526%E2%80%931867) "Kingdom of Hungary (1526–1867)") and [Bohemia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bohemia "Kingdom of Bohemia"), several junior branches of the House of Habsburg-Lorraine reigned in the Italian duchies of Tuscany (until 1737-1796, 1814-1860), [Parma](/wiki/Duchy_of_Parma_and_Piacenza "Duchy of Parma and Piacenza") (1814-1847) and [Modena](/wiki/Duchy_of_Modena_and_Reggio "Duchy of Modena and Reggio") (1814-1859). Another member of the house, Archduke [Maximilian of Austria](/wiki/Maximilian_I_of_Mexico "Maximilian I of Mexico"), was [Emperor of Mexico](/wiki/Emperor_of_Mexico "Emperor of Mexico") (1863–67).

In 1900, [Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria](/wiki/Archduke_Franz_Ferdinand_of_Austria "Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria") (then [heir presumptive](/wiki/Heir_presumptive "Heir presumptive") to the [Austro-Hungarian](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") throne) contracted a [morganatic marriage](/wiki/Morganatic_marriage "Morganatic marriage") with Countess [Sophie Chotek](/wiki/Sophie,_Duchess_of_Hohenberg "Sophie, Duchess of Hohenberg"). Their descendants, known as the [House of Hohenberg](/wiki/House_of_Hohenberg "House of Hohenberg"), have been excluded from succession to the Austro-Hungarian crown, but not that of Lorraine, where morganatic marriage has never been outlawed. Nevertheless, [Otto von Habsburg](/wiki/Otto_von_Habsburg "Otto von Habsburg"), the eldest grandson of Franz Ferdinand's [younger brother](/wiki/Archduke_Otto_Francis_of_Austria "Archduke Otto Francis of Austria"), was universally regarded as the head of the house until his death in 2011.[[20]](#cite_note-20) It was at [Nancy](/wiki/Nancy,_France "Nancy, France"), the former capital of the House of Vaudémont, that the former crown prince married [Princess Regina of Saxe-Meiningen](/wiki/Princess_Regina_of_Saxe-Meiningen "Princess Regina of Saxe-Meiningen") in 1951.[[2]](#cite_note-Shepherd-2)

* [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II,_Duke_of_Lorraine "Charles II, Duke of Lorraine"), r. 1390–1431

Charles II died without male heir, the duchy passing to [Isabella, Duchess of Lorraine](/wiki/Isabella,_Duchess_of_Lorraine "Isabella, Duchess of Lorraine"), consort of Naples by marriage to Duke [René of Anjou](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_of_Anjou "René of Anjou"). The duchy passed to their son [John II](/wiki/John_II,_Duke_of_Lorraine "John II, Duke of Lorraine") (r. 1453–1470), whose son [Nicholas I](/wiki/Nicholas_I,_Duke_of_Lorraine "Nicholas I, Duke of Lorraine") (r. 1470–1473) died without heir. The title now went to Nicholas' aunt (sister of John II) [Yolande](/wiki/Yolande,_Duchess_of_Lorraine "Yolande, Duchess of Lorraine").

### Heads of the junior ducal line

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Lorraine&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Heads of the junior ducal line")]

The junior ducal line of the House of Lorraine was formed by Yolande's marriage to [Frederick II, Count of Vaudémont](/wiki/Frederick_II,_Count_of_Vaud%C3%A9mont "Frederick II, Count of Vaudémont") (1428–1470), a member of the cadet (Vaudémont) branch of the senior ducal line, who was descended from [John I](/wiki/John_I,_Duke_of_Lorraine "John I, Duke of Lorraine") (Yolande's great-grandfather) via his younger son [Frederick I, Count of Vaudémont](/wiki/Frederick_I,_Count_of_Vaud%C3%A9mont "Frederick I, Count of Vaudémont") (1346–1390), [Antoine, Count of Vaudémont](/wiki/Antoine,_Count_of_Vaud%C3%A9mont "Antoine, Count of Vaudémont") (c. 1395–1431) and [Frederick II, Count of Vaudémont](/wiki/Frederick_II,_Count_of_Vaud%C3%A9mont "Frederick II, Count of Vaudémont") (1417–1470). René inherited the title of Duke of Lorraine upon his marriage in 1473.
